speaker: interviewer
text: Szprycer. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Amazing, amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: This has to be told.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. I know. This is really good.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what [? Serge ?] told you, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: He tell me everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Serge ?] went mad, he-- when he heard it, only a little bit. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Serge ?] called and said, you have to, you have to. So we ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] and [? Serge-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were at [INAUDIBLE], at the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's going to shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So he was standing there. He probably had-- they all drunk, you know. They all had alcohol. He was standing in front of-- I was standing a few yards away from him. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: He took the pistol out of his what's it called, out of his-- and he was holding it, like this. And I was standing. And you know what I did?

speaker: interviewee
text: [STOMPS] [SPEAKING GERMAN] And I said, oh yeah, but you can image how I felt. And I stayed. And I stayed. And he put his hands slowly down. I remember that hand. And he put his pistol back into his what's it called. And I went-- [SIGHS]. If I would have run, I wouldn't have lived. If I would have run away, he would have shot. This was their sadism. I stayed. I put, and he probably-- you see, psychologically, now I think about it, they-- they must have thought, and they must have talked about me in the officer club. That Berliner, [GERMAN], he's unbelievable. He suffers, and he suffers, and he suffers, and he's still able to-- to-- to take that all. They had respect for me, in one way. But the-- [INAUDIBLE] respect, they could have maybe put me into the gas chamber later. I don't know. Until that day, it was-- and I did-- I was the [GERMAN]. You know, I was what's it called. I played the-- the-- the strong man. And you can imagine, I'm not tall now. I was smaller. [LAUGHS] I had that uniform on with-- with boots, you know, clean. Every morning, when I came to that gate, I put-- every night, in the barracks, I used to put my trousers under the mattress to put my-- that perfect looking the next day. Cleaned my teeth-- I went into the cold-- we had the potatoes kitchen there-- cleaned my-- I showered myself with cold water, not to get lice. So when I came to the gate, my boots, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [GERMAN] 875 [INAUDIBLE] reports on the gate. You can imagine, I want to see that [INAUDIBLE]. I can imagine, I would have been in their position, this is-- this is unbelievable. After four or five months, I got stronger and stronger. The day came-- very, very powerful story, telling that the day came July, August. I didn't see the other boys. Sometimes I saw a group. They were living in the back, with the Sonderkommando. And [INAUDIBLE] said, always, he said that later, and I know I gave-- when I saw these boys, I gave them bread. I was organized. I was outside. I helped them. But in the evening, they were closed into the barracks. But I-- [SNEEZES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Bless you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the barracks, and I was-- I could walk about. And no-- nobody came to the front gate. The day-- Rabetzki was also a bastard. Rabetzki was the worst one of all. [SNEEZES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you hear the name-- name. He was Romanian SS. He got-- he's-- in all the books, he is. When I look at the books, I said to my wife, this is Rabetzki! And the name was underneath, Rabetzki. My wife listens all the films lately. And then she hears, [? Schwarzhuber. ?] She said, Hellmuth, you told me all these stories-- Kramer. So I said, definitely. It was-- this was-- I don't have to read the books for that. The day came, there was in July and August the big meeting of Himmler, that's the [GERMAN]-- the high-- high rank officers. On the crematoriums, like you go there now, they had all seats put there. And they had a meeting. Now I know what that meeting was. Then, I didn't know. That was a meeting Hitler ordered that it should be-- go quicker, the gassing. It went too-- too slow. But we had to march with the orchestra, with the band there. And I marched along with Rabetzki there. And there was a whole-- I don't know who it was, all the-- [GERMAN]. They were all there. Himmler, I saw also, with his glasses. It's written in books that he was there. And I whistled the march music with them. They didn't-- they didn't do that, you know. So when the march music was played, finished, and I whistled, then it finished. They played other music. And they were [? discussing ?] that from the-- it had this earlier-- a lot, that it had to go quicker. I walked about. I walked about.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I-- now, I was-- when I make the film, I'll show you-- show it. And then I saw a little-- like a little villa, in the back there. You know, a little-- a little boy. He's new. And-- he's-- he likes to find out. He searches. I didn't know the danger I was in. And I walked over a little bridge. And a man was standing on the door. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I went in there. I said, [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, come-- come in. So after-- this is the gas chamber. And I went in. We walked a bit. And he showed me the metal door what he came out. And I said, is that not [GERMAN]? There's-- there's still blood there. He said, yes, because it has to be cleaned up now. Because each time they have a other transport coming in, they had to clean it. Because otherwise, these people would have known. These people went into these things thinking for-- not that they'd be gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him, oy. I said, [GERMAN]. And you know what happens? They come in from the other side. I was at the back. Because they came in from the front, they went through the-- through the hole, where they have to put off their clothes. That I know now. They had to hang their clothes on-- they had hangers there. It says there you're going to take showers. Sit down. Take it easy. The SS were standing there, taking kids on their arms. And you go. Later on, you come out. You go into the [GERMAN]. That happened before. But I was standing at what-- what-- what finishes. So he says to me, they come in here and it's full up to the top. The metal-- the clo-- I don't-- he was-- every three months, they kill the Sonderkommando. And then we close the door here, too. And then he said, 10, 15 minutes, you hear them [? shying ?] and shouting and-- and after 10, 15 minutes, he said, they open the door again, it's finished. Nobody. And then you see all the strong people on top and all the weak people on  the bottom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the strong ones on the top, he says-- they have stomachs full of air, or something. You see people with scratched on the walls, because they want to get on top of each other, the last death fight. You know, the fight for-- against the death, see. And he said, the SS goes in there, before we have to work, so with their carabiners, with the knives. And they pushed it into the stomachs, that there was should go out-- the air, and then the blood comes out, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were already flying out when the door opened up. But he said then-- I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? You take them out with the hands then, or what-- what? No, no, he says. You see these scissors here? Big scissors, where they had to be put round their-- what's it called? Necks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Necks-- and they pushed them out like this, he said. And come here [GERMAN], come here [GERMAN]. Did he know I'm going to live? Was that some kind of holy man? Come here, [GERMAN]. Here, as you see that place, there we take out the gold teeth. We break them out. And by women, we have to look into their-- how do you call it, if they nothing hidden there, in between their legs there. And do you see that fire thing here? Do you see that arm? There they are put on and put in. And on my life, today I visit that and-- in July-- and if one of the SS men would have seen me-- Rabetzki-- that I went in there and went out of there, I wouldn't have been here. So I looked into the gas chambers when they were working. Today, I can't believe it. I can't believe it. I said, that's-- [? Serge ?] also said to me is that-- I said, yes. Because when I went with the group from the Belgian television, and I said that I go in there, and I was not found out, so that it must be-- I must be-- God must have-- somebody was with me. And that Sonderkommando-- he-- he's not alive. After three months, they killed him as well. Because I spoke with them. I was in contact with then, even when they went out the gates. But I never-- but that moment, where they had that talk about gassing the people quicker, and I moved around there, they didn't see me-- quick in and out, [INAUDIBLE] that. Then I knew. I went back to the barracks with the band. And Rabetzki, the biggest sadist-- he used to hit me. Oh, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He gave me-- when we came into the camp, he said, Berliner, come here. Here is a box of chocolate for you, [GERMAN]. You whistled in front of the marvelous-- in front of the [GERMAN]. Suddenly-- so my life went on in D lager. And in the evening, when the barracks were closed, that was-- you see, in these-- in these camps, the SS had everything. The-- they had their own tailor shop there, the [GERMAN]. All the uniforms the SS used was made to measure. All the boots they had-- they had the most beautiful boots maker. Some, they didn't gas those people they-- they needed. Jewelry-- the ones who made jewelry, the ones expert you have to check the diamonds-- probably, they-- they were all gassed after a time. And the ones who made the boots for them, that was all made to measure. So they had a terrific life. Music they had as as well. One evening, they want to listen to Bach and something. They came and they said, come play for us. And that-- so day by day went on. Day by day, we suffered. People died. The gas smell. The Gypsy transport went into the gas chamber, crying the whole night, these children. Nobody talks about Gypsies that was also treated. Polish people-- some of them were hanged because they did sabotage. But they were-- I've never heard of a Pole going into the gas chamber. I never heard anybody else who was not Jewish. The only ones I heard was Gypsies and the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: The day came when we were on the gate. Moll-- [GERMAN] Moll. [GERMAN] Moll was the [? chief ?] on the crematoriums. In the end of '44, he came up to the gate. And he said to [GERMAN] Schwarzhuber, I want to have a [GERMAN] as well. And he says, can I have one? And we were three. He says, I want the Berliner. So he said, the Berliner, you don't get, he said. The-- you-- [LAUGHS] that the Berliner-- can you imagine? I heard that the Berliner stays here. So they-- I was-- and you know, they-- they-- they knew I did a good job, as a child, cleaning and all that. If you have got somebody who does a good job-- so I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I said, I didn't want to go with him, yeah? So he said, you know what I'm going to do? Moll-- have you heard of Moll? M-O double L. He was the crematorium [? chief, ?] the biggest sadist of all. And he used to the womens in the what's it called-- and a fat man, a butcher. Most of the people were killed from him, gassed from him. He was the big man, the butcher. He said, you know, the Russian and the-- the Yid-- the Helmut, the-- that the Berliner-- when I-- let them fight. We gamble. Let them fight. The one who wins goes with me to Gleiwitz. And the one who doesn't win stay here. But, you know, I hated that Ukraine. He was not-- he reported me many times. He was a real bastard, a real anti-Semite-- as a young kid, even, you know? So I must have given a real hitting, you know? We boxed. And I must have put him down like I-- so Moll said I won. I-- I didn't like it. I went into the camp. And I had friends in the orchestra. The one who plays the flute was a watchmaker. The one who played the flute was a terrific-- I said-- he was like my little father. I said to him, listen, I-- I should go with Moll to-- to Gleiwitz? He said, Hellmuth, if you can get out of Birkenau, get out. Here, later, probably, the gas. But Birkenau, there's no gas chamber. Go with him. Do you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Birkenau no gas chamber, the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or the other. No in Birkenau-- in Gleiwitz, there's no gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moll was given Gleiwitz camp as a commandant for the good work he did. He was-- how do you call it? Saluted. And he was promoted for-- I didn't know. I went with the-- with the van to Gleiwitz. And that must have been in January-- the end, the beginning. And we went there. And in January, I was on [GERMAN] there, on the gate. But I didn't last very long. The Russians were shooting. And the-- you had the guns and everything. One morning, we woke up. There was no more Moll, no commandants. The-- most of the officer fled. We were by ourself-- all by ourself. But the Ukraines SS, and this little bastard, this real-- you know, the ordinary sergeants, they didn't let us stay. They didn't let-- they said, you have to march now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been in February-- February or March, something like that. And March-- yeah, March.

speaker: interviewee
text: The death march. '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: '45, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had the death march to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day, it was also cold still. You  know, it was winter. That was freezing. They had to get us above-- across a bridge. Because if they didn't get us across the bridge, the Russians could have catch up with us. They didn't allow that. But I was on that march. I couldn't-- I couldn't anymore. My friend said, come [? Hellmuthchin. ?] It's not long anymore. Many people on that march-- old people died by walking. We went through Blechhammer. Blechhammer, there were many people who was apart, parted from us. Some went to the left, some to the right. Some went back to Gleiwitz. But we went to Gross-Rosen. We continued. That march-- I tell you something. That is something, the worst happened to me at that march, because there was no more [GERMAN]. There was no more food. There was no more-- as much as we had at that camp water and something, there happened things of you never can believe. They never took us on the streets in the winter. They took us through the fields, because the SS had to go on the streets. They were running away from the Russians, or going to the Russians-- I don't know what happened there-- for military use. And we had a wagon with a horse, which the SS put their-- their belongings on. You know that the horse, in the middle of the what's it called, on the fields, so high snow, suddenly that horse couldn't push anymore. So they took the horse on the back. And the prisoners-- we had to do the-- the what's it called, do it. People were-- anybody who was sitting down to take a-- a commission, to shit or piss or something, they couldn't get up. Because once you took your hands-- you didn't have gloves. You didn't have really shoe. Once you sit down, they couldn't get up, from the cold. So the SS, you didn't hear nothing else but poof-- shooting-- poof. When you looked back, all dead bodies on that road, lying, on the-- on the-- on that march-- all dead, all dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we said hun-- we said, food, food. [INAUDIBLE]. We came into a little-- little village. They put us into a what's it called. It's written in the books, even-- into a little farmhouse-- ice cold. We didn't have any-- you know, marching. And they must have given us pork soup, you know, [GERMAN]. Everybody got diarrhea. We shit, and we shit, because we-- we didn't eat for days.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, in the [GERMAN, you know, where the [GERMAN] they came in. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And you know, not one, they went with their [? carbines, ?] with their knives, into the thing. If this was somebody left, that one little Jew, they didn't want to leave behind. There was-- that was all kinds and Yugoslavs-- SS. But one little Jew, they would not-- finally, [INAUDIBLE] say, come. We go again on the march.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went through little villages. I don't know what those villages were-- [GERMAN], you know? People ca-- stood on their houses, looking at us. There was not one person who gave us a piece of dry bread, no matter, or an apple or something-- not one of them. I can say that when I see these-- when I got through these, I will do that one day-- go through this village and ask some of these-- not one. We marched, and marched, and marched, until we got to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got into Gross-Rosen, we must have left maybe with 12,000 or 15,000 people. We must have arrived with 2,000. A little ago, when we got them there, there were all people in there. That camp was already from the officers. Everybody left that camp empty. There were people there from all different parts. They were on a death march that was-- empt-- there was no office-- but it was not empty from-- from prisoners. We came all there to be sorted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they slung us all in there. High-- there was snow like that, and also dirt, like that. When you walked in there, you lost your shoes. You had to put on the shoes again. The put us into these barracks, put hundreds of people. We were lying next to each other like this, next like this, I said to you. [INAUDIBLE] press. And everybody who came in first into the room took the first place to lie down. And if you came, they said, no, this is mine! People were fighting. There I saw people eating dead bodies, in Gross-Rosen. The ones who were lying there, I saw them cutting up the-- the legs, and they were chewing on it. We didn't have food. We didn't have nothing. These people were dying, like hell. And-- and that death march-- that's the most horrible thing which happened. There, I thought, I'm not going to over live that. There, I said, finish [GERMAN]. No, my dear boy, I said. This is finished. People got typhus, diarrhea. I-- I always walked about with diarrhea. I don't know how I could have stand it, you know? I did, always.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, suddenly, from Gross-Rosen, we were separated. Some went to Buchenwald. And I went to Leitmeritz. When I came into Leitmeritz, it wasn't far from there. We came into the barracks. You heard a lot of [INAUDIBLE] already. And people were dying there from typhus, typhus. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I have to over live this. I have to over live this. I went out. And I went up to a officer. And I said, I was a [GERMAN] But Leitmeritz was not a concentration camp with gas chambers. They were Wehrmachts there-- Wehrmachts. There were no SS. They were Wehrmachts. They kept the camp. I went in there. And I saw the commandant with his horse, riding about. And [INAUDIBLE]-- idiots. They must have been-- I don't know-- to show off and [INAUDIBLE]. And I saw him. I spoke to him. I said, [GERMAN]. I knew the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He said to the SS guard that was on the gate-- the SS were still guards. But they were officers, Wehrmachts. He said, send them to my office. [LAUGHS] He sent me to his office. And there, the first time in my life, he gave me-- he said [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Sit down, my dear boy. He was [GERMAN]-- not the [GERMAN]. He was a Wehrmacht officer, a high rank-- a Wehrmacht officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, [GERMAN], you know what I'll do? I'll give you a job in the kitchen-- in the SS kitchen, not in the-- would you like that? Oh, yeah, did [INAUDIBLE] was definite here. So I worked there, in the SS kitchen, like not for months, or six weeks, or so-- I was eating like a horse, you know? Well, you can imagine. They had-- I-- I cleaned-- in the stable, I cleaned his horses. I did all that work, until the day came we had to get out and march back to what's it called. We marched back to foot, to Theresienstadt I came back to Theresienstadt. I came back to Theresienstadt and to the gate. And people were waiting for us there, to see if some of their families haven't come back. That was in-- maybe in March '45 or something. And my-- I-- suddenly, I saw my aunt from Hamburg, [GERMAN] Anna, one of the daughters. She was oh-- Hellmuth. And my cousins, Peter [? and Erne, ?] they were gassed. They didn't come back-- her children. She came on the end, being [GERMAN] on the end. And I saw her. And I went up to her. And I went where she lived. And I said, [GERMAN], can't I live with you? Oh, she said. In here it's not possible. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us into the Hamburg [GERMAN]. In the Hamburg [GERMAN], people were dying like flies from typhus-- like flies! Wherever you walked was death, smell. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Again, I am not-- and Theresienstadt, I knew. So I escaped from the Hamburg [GERMAN]. My aunt didn't want to take me, so I lived in the what's it called, in cafe house. I slept-- I know the cafe house. I slept on the bed there. But I felt that it was coming to the end of the war. I felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in-- and I met a boy there that Hellmuth, you knew me before the camp, [INAUDIBLE]-- a Czech boy. I don't know, he was a mission-- he said, my mother has come to fetch me. So your mother is going to fetch you? How can she-- so your mother can't fetch you. It's not finished, the war. Yeah, yeah, she has somebody. I said, please ask your mother to take her with me. One morning came a post wagon there, from the [GERMAN]. And his mother came. And he said, momma, come on, this is Hellmuth Szprycer from [GERMAN]. Can we not take him with us? And she took me with her. We got into that van. We were hidden under blankets. And we were off. And I was happy. Oh, I was [INAUDIBLE] happy. Oh. In the middle of the way, we got stopped. [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. Stopped-- the thing in the back went open. We went under these blankets. You can imagine how I felt. They opened the blankets. And I was-- and it was Czech partisans. Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They saw my number. They took me into their hands. They kissed me. They kissed me. They-- they pressed me. This is probably the first Auschwitz [GERMAN] they saw. They took us to a town, a little village, with his mother. And she spoke Czech to them. I couldn't speak Czech in the moment, so-- and she-- and they took us to-- into-- into restaurant. And they gave us a feast of-- with a lot of people there. They-- the Czech partisans went quick around. And they said, when you go to Czechoslovakia-- to Prague, be careful-- to this woman, to the woman. The Germans are still there. The Panzer division hasn't left. The bastards were still there. Take Hellmuth into your flats and hide him there. And don't come out until the liberation will be. We went to Prague. I haven't had time to look up. Maybe that will-- we went up in a beautiful, modern flat, in the third floor. I lived there. I saw the Germans, the [GERMAN]. They didn't leave. Every day, on the radio, the Americans were saying-- the English, we are coming, we are coming. Nobody came. But then came the day, suddenly, the Panzer division left Prague. But they left it with chi-- children in front of the canons. They went out that they shouldn't be shot at. And then, when they left, they said to us, you can come out of the apartments. The Russians are here. The Russian came near. I must admit that the Russians, on that point-- the Germans were going over to the American frontier, to give themselves there. Because the Americans didn't suffer. And the English  didn't suffer. But they knew that they had to run away from the Russians. That we have to agree, with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I do? I went down. They used all the Germans on the street. I helped them to put the hot coals on the back. I drank vodka with the Russians. They came there with their little wagons, with-- with their horses, with their Cossacks trousers, with their balalaikas. They put their horses on the cemeteries. For-- they-- ordinary boys, but we kissed each other. And we danced. And we sang [RUSSIAN]. We sang. And we-- and for me, it was freedom. And I saw the German who were captured. They had to work on the street with the SS. And I shouted and I spitted. There I did a bit of my what's it called-- of my hatred. And I spoke to the officers, which came with the tanks. The day came that I said to the woman, it's very nice that you helped me, but I have to go. I have to move on. What do you mean, you move on? I have to go to America. [INAUDIBLE], whatever-- [INAUDIBLE] America. I-- I knew my grandmother said he was in America. [INAUDIBLE] America-- Argentina, I don't-- I-- so I went into the what's it called, onto the train station park. Everywhere, I showed my number. They let me pass. I got on the train. We were on the train with two French mens. And suddenly, the train is stopped at night. Stop, [NON-ENGLISH]. Look [INAUDIBLE]-- outside, the Russians at the car windows. [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a [INAUDIBLE] of Russian. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And these two Frenchmen [INAUDIBLE] me, as well, and they pushed us. And I said, what is this here? Is it going to start again? What is this here? The-- they took us into the country there, a little village there, into a villa. I could-- then, I spoke good Russian. In front of a-- of a officer, of a high rank officer-- he was sitting fat there, drinking his vodka. And I went up to him. I showed him my number. I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I said to him, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I had to go with these two soldiers, and the two Frenchmen, on the other side of the villa, into a house. That I didn't like, you know. I suffered too much as that is. So what happened is we went up the-- the attic. They took us up the attic. The French mens also said, what's this here? What's this? We went up. And we saw a little woman on the right. The Russians were there singing and drinking vodka. I said, early at the morning, at 3 o'clock, in English, I piss off. I said, I'm going to-- I'm going to escape here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went down the stairs. I looked at the room. They were all sleeping there on the beds, you know-- on the tables. [SNORES] And I passed, you know. And I went on the street, early in the morning-- light. I saw a Czechoslovakian there. I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. He showed me the way. He said, this way. I walked on the street with my little baggage. I was singing. I was a free man. I ran. I ran. I walked. I ran. I was happy-- to America. [COUGHS] [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I went-- suddenly finished. The train was stopped at that moment when they made the-- the separate zones-- American zone, Russian zone. Czechoslovakia was made in two. I come in the gate. First time my life, I see the American uniforms. They look much better [INAUDIBLE] the Russians were describing. So I said, what I'm going to do now? I was standing there. But I can't-- English I can't-- I couldn't speak English nothing. So I start singing that song from my sister, when she used to play at the parties at home, at the-- with the records.

speaker: interviewee
text: (SINGING) A tisket, a tasket-- jeepers creepers--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Americans sang it with me. And-- and they looked at the Russians. [LAUGHS] And the Russians looked at them. And they said, let him come over to us. He opened the gate. And I was over by the American side. So when I got to the American side, that Jeep-- they had that Jeep there. And I got into that Jeep. And they were driving us till it was-- we were-- we were-- I was not-- I was never what's it called-- I trusted nobody. Driving-- where did they go? Suddenly into big gate. And they opened that big gate. They got in there. All the German officers, the army, was in there.
